Alright, we're moving to step three now, your USP. So the way I define USP is a little bit differently than a lot of other people do. I think the original use of the term for those of you who know anything about advertising history is unique selling proposition, USP. For the way that I work, that wasn't enough. The original USP was done back in the 50s by I think Rosser Reeves, a gentleman who was a minor god in the advertising world. But he was dealing with slogans and he was selling cigarettes and soap in magazines and on TV. So when he was talking about a unique selling proposition or a unique sales proposition, he was talking about, in a way, he was talking about branding and he was talking about a different world where you tried to position yourself among other competitors in a particular way in particular markets. For me, I change it around to P-U-S actually which spells pus but don't worry about that. It's kind of funny but basically the USP is you want to position yourself uniquely in your market in order to sell. Position yourself uniquely in your market in order to sell. You're not positioning yourself to be the funniest guy in the market or the smartest guy in the market or anything. You're positioning yourself in your market uniquely. In other words, you're not trying to be just like everyone else. You're not trying to be just the same old, same old. You're not copying what everybody else does. So you are being a little unique but you're doing all of this in order to sell. Now we'll go deeper into that. Your elevator chat is something we like to talk about which is I like to ask clients, imagine getting into an elevator at the ninth floor and someone who is in your perfect target audience, your perfect avatar, your perfect prospect walks in and they say, hi, what do you do? Or they just come in and you know that because say you're at a hotel where there's a conference and you know that everybody at the hotel is in your prospect range. So what would you say while the elevator is going from the ninth floor to the ground floor which is a matter of seconds, what would you say when meeting a perfect prospect? And the thing is a lot of people screw this up. They can't do it. They need minutes. They need 15 minutes. They can't explain what they do very succinctly no matter how much time they have. They kind of garble it. They aren't sure of how to introduce themselves and you need to get this down. So the reason we call it an elevator chat is to think about how quickly this has to happen to a prospect who doesn't know who you are, who isn't interested in who you are, who really is not happy maybe to be talking with you in the first place and may want to bolt and tell they find out that you have something that can help them. So you want to think about who you are, what do you do or sell, how long have you been in this business, how successful have you been and how do you help customers? So let me go back to this. So I might say, hi, my name is John Carlton for the last 30 years. I've been writing ads for clients that can revolutionize the sales process and turn a moribund launch or ad campaign into something stellar that brings in a lot of money. So right there I've explained who I am, what it is I do or sell, how long I've been in the business, how I help customers. That kind of short, pithy delivery is what you want to aim for. So there's a difference between the company line, which is what most clients believe, it's why clients believe people buy versus the sales detective research that we talked about which will tell you why people actually buy. So the company line that I used to encounter was business owners who thought they knew why people were buying their product or service. They were often wrong because they thought it was the price. This happened a lot. They thought I am the bargain king or I am the most expensive plumber on the block or some kind of thing that set them apart monetarily. When it turned out that their clients would have paid a lot more and one of the things I used to do as a marketing consultant was I would tell clients to double their prices right off the bat. Have you tested doubling your price? Have you tested it at all? And usually they had not and they actually found out that, wow, people were willing to pay a lot more than they had thought they were willing to pay or pay a lot less but you get more customers. There's a lot of things that go into this. When you do your research, when you understand why people actually buy, you can get away from what I call the company line, which is a lot of times companies just believe that people buy from them for some reason and then this becomes gospel. This becomes cemented into their thinking and they don't, facts just kind of are repelled from their brain. So you need to get to the truth of why it is people actually buy. Now the corporate line is often very boring whereas a direct hit is very sexy. When you think about the golf ads or about the riding buck naked on a clapped out two stroke, that is kind of sexy. That is, when I say sexy, I mean it's just kind of reverberates. It wakes people up and we're going to be talking about that a lot more as we get into the actual riding. We're still in the preparation stage right here. But the corporate line, if you look to the way most businesses position themselves, they're very conservative and they're just downright boring and that's something you don't want to do. It's very important to know it's not about you at all. The prospect doesn't care who you are except in how who you are affects them, how it can make their problems better, how it can solve situations that they're in. So you've got to take yourself out of the equation and apply a lot of empathy here to the USP. So it's not what you sell but what you sell does for your customer. That's the most important thing. That's going to position you uniquely. If you're the only person who has the secrets of training Doberman Pinchers how to be cuddly little lap dogs and you really can say from years and years in the biz that you're the only one who's known how to do this, then that sets you up uniquely. That's what you do. You're not someone who sells training materials. You're the secret to having my Doberman be the most wonderful dog in the world. A very, very different thing. You want to aim for id ticklers as we call it. Do you present something that helps me do something faster, easier, simpler? Is it a better bargain, an unfair advantage? Does it provide inside information? Is there privilege and luxury involved or bragging rights? For example, two neighbors come home with the same identical car and they pull into the driveway and they just bought the car. Who wins that neighborhood little battle of who got the better deal? Is it the guy who paid less? The guy who got a better long lasting deal like with service and maybe he had gas paid for him for the next two years? Or is it the guy that got a just plain got a better deal on paying for everything and got it all tricked out with everything else? Bragging rights are very, very important. A lot of people get very close to buying and don't pull the trigger because they're afraid they're going to make a mistake. That's something that you really have to understand what's going on. When you say you can do things faster, easier, simpler, this is a better bargain than you would have gotten before. These are very, very important ways to position yourself amongst a crowded market where other people are saying, yeah, we can do it faster. But really it's not. You can do it faster than they can. It's easier. It's simpler. It's a better bargain. Remember you will need to prove everything immediately to a very skeptical reader. When you're positioning yourself within your market specifically to get some sales messaging done, you have to understand that the average prospect, even your perfect prospect is kind of waiting with one foot behind him to bolt. They really are looking for a reason to not deal with you at all. The best thing you can do to them today is to give them a reason to forget about you, to give them a reason to not care about what you have or how much it is or how easy or faster or whatever it is. That's what they really want. You actually present a problem to them when you have a solution. You actually take what can often be the cinders of hope where they may think they've never going to find the solution to whatever is bugging them, to better sales, to losing weight, to having a more bubbly personality. Whatever their problem is that they're trying to solve that you have the answer to, they are going to be skeptical. They've probably even burned before. They've probably tried other stuff. There's all kinds of reasons why they really don't want to talk to you. He has to keep that in mind. Think in terms of a non-clever slogan as you're going along. I call myself the most respected and ripped off copywriter alive. That's not saying I'm the best copywriter who's ever been in existence. That's not saying I'm the highest grossing. That's not saying a lot of things. It's saying what's true. When I ripped off, I mean that people have been taking my ads and using them as templates for their own ads for a decade or longer. I'm very respected because I've spoken on stages all over the world. People do look to me for advice. That's a slogan that I have. It separates me from other people in my market. It gives me a unique bend or a unique angle here. We're pointing at all times towards actually getting something done, either buying my services, getting involved with me into my world at some point. Do not deepen the mystery. Solve it with specifics. Who are you? Why do you do what you do? What are the results that you get? How is this going to help me, the prospect, when I encounter you? We're going to get into something very specific here in a minute. This is the starter formula I want you to think about. We help this group of people do this benefit or benefits better. That's a starter formula. We help dentists in Los Angeles get more paying clients without having to rely on insurance. We do it faster and without charging a lot of money for the service. Something like that. We help and then whatever the group of people that you've identified going back to the avatar do this benefit or benefits better. It could be better, faster, easier, simpler, less expensive. The positioning addition that you want to do this is we help this group of people do this benefit better, better than the competitor or common wisdom. You can get better results with us than you can with company XYZ and company ABC. You can get better service from us than the other company in town who is trying to service these particular kinds of engines or cars or heating equipment or whatever. Or common wisdom. We can help you do something faster than you ever believe possible. We can help you get a degree and a certain subject faster than most people can get a single year's education. We can do whatever it is that you have better, faster than. Then finally, this is the benefit addition. Even F and then give the worst situation possible. We can help overweight people finally get down to the set weight point that they've really wanted faster than any other diet ever tried. Even if you've tried tons of diets, been disappointed before or never actually lost weight and kept it off in your life. This is the USP paradigm. This is how you get to that point where you're actually going to uniquely position yourself in the market in order to sell. We help this group of people do this benefit or benefits better than competitor or common wisdom even if worst situation possible. So I want to give a little warning too. Do not fall in love with your USP. It will not sell on its own. It is merely a way to set up interest and get your foot in the door. It is however also the foundation of your best headlines. So here's an example of going after what is a fairly crowded market which would be information for guitarists who want to get better. There's a lot of information out there, a lot of teaching DVDs, a lot of books and a lot of film on the subject of how to become a better guitar player. But by knowing what it was that a lot of the people who learned to play guitar and want to get better wanted, I was able to craft a different kind of headline. So this one based on the USP which is the music industry's secret weapon, guitar wizard, finally reveals his embarrassingly easy shortcuts that can take anyone from raw rookie to stage ready, quote, get all the girls, unquote, guitarist as fast as humanly possible. And down here in the subhead we talk about he's staking his industry wide reputation on the fact that he can take someone with no previous skills, no musical experience and no clue how to even tune a guitar and have them ready to play and jam on a complete set list of over 100 songs just by mastering a few lessons. So what this is going after is the guitarist who dreams, who has big dreams of going on stage. They may never go on stage but that is why they're playing guitar. They're thinking if I could play guitar at a better level then maybe I could be the hero, maybe I could be the person on stage playing with the band where people are looking at me and I'm in the center of the action. So by firing that up, by understanding what the market wanted, we're able to uniquely position this guy among all other teachers who were saying things like I'll just make you be the best guitarist you can or become a professional level guitarist or whatever. This brings it down into reality. In the golf world, knowing as I explained before that a lot of golfers can't break 100 to save their lives, they're arthritic, they're at bad backs, they're older. So this ad was about a golf DVD that was going to teach some basic how to play golf better tactics. So the headline is who else wants another 40 to 60 effortless yards on every tee shot starting with your very next drive, dot, dot, dot, without trying or changing your swing or practicing or even pretending to be in better shape. So that is just a litany that addresses all of the problems that people have when they're looking at other golf products or other situations where they can learn about how to be a better golfer. They don't want to practice. They're not going to get in better shape. They've got a beer belly and their back has been bothering them for a long time and the arthritis is kicking up all the time. And they don't want to change their swing. I don't know how many of you are golfers, but golfers, the main advice that professionals give to golfers who just want to be better golfers is go out and practice a lot. Hit lots, practice more than you actually play and spend a lot of time on the practice range and nobody wants to hear that. I certainly don't. I'm a bad golfer and I'm very happy with my golfing, but I got good enough to be able to hold my head up and play with all of my friends and occasionally win. So I don't practice. I'm not going to do the things that a lot of golf professionals ask me to do to get in better shape and I'm older. And the idea of having 40 to 60 effortless yards, another 40 to 60 is revolutionary. That puts me into being a better driver off the tee than all of my buddies that I play with. I want that. So that's the kind of thing that USP allows you to get into. And that's the summation of the USP. So there's an exercise for this. I think you're really going to enjoy it. This is one of those steps that a lot of people got to and light bulbs really started going off. Again, as you probably noticed, we haven't started writing yet as far as actually writing the sales messages because we're still building up the foundation from which every other step is going to move from. And we are actually at the point where the next step we will start looking at the things that will get you into the actual writing. And it gets really fun and it starts going really fast now. So I'll see you at the next lesson. All right. Welcome back to the Q&A session to follow the USP. What you're going to see is this really is as simple as John just made it sound. So there are only a couple of questions I think are worth clarifying. And this is one of those situations where simple really works so you can get to it as soon as possible. Okay. One question we've seen over and over again. People ask, especially if they're new to this or the business is growing, is how many USPs can they have? Do they have to have just one? It's a USP is an evolving thing. The USP you have when you start out, if you're in a business, if you're a rookie business person, or it's your first business or the business at a certain level, your USP will be a certain way as you grow, as competition changes, as the playing field changes, your USP can change. That's why I said don't fall in love with your USP. This is something that can you can change it every day. You can keep refining it. I will tell you that top marketers and really good writers are constantly fussing with their USP. They are constantly changing about what is it? That's more unique. How is this change? New information sometimes comes in. You may make an assumption that price is the biggest deal. You may operate on that. It may be supported by everybody you talk to. All the sales people, all of the other people, even the competition may support this. And then a piece of information comes in where, oops, price is no longer an issue or price has changed as an issue. So therefore you can go back. So fuss with it. But it's so simple that as you change it, because it's interlocking pieces, part of it could change, all of it could change. But by knowing this, this is the font from which all other things spring. Yeah, there you go. I think that's one of the important points to get people now. Because we'll see them sometimes doing the exercises or asking questions. They're trying to get like, okay, now that I'm maybe doing something like writing, they just overkill this. Yes. Just keep in mind that there's more to come. And this might, while you did mention in the lecture that we're not writing yet, there is a chance this could go into an ad or something much like this. So we're really on the precipice. However, by overcomplicating it or trying to put more in the USP than you need in the USP, you can make this step harder than it needs to be. Yes. Simple is better. Yes. So this is what I get. Just simple, better. Again, you have a limited amount of time to deliver this message. This is not the time to sit someone down and say, well, I need to think about it. 30 minutes of your time and this will be clear to you. So, Pithy, let me tell you a story. Yeah. Before I tell you what I do, I need to tell you what it was like growing up on the farm. That's not the time for this. There may never be a time, but this is certainly not it. Okay. Let's say I'm selling into a market or, you know, my avatar, I believe they, when they talk among themselves and what they're doing, they use big words or they use very specific lingo or language that the average person has no idea what they're talking about. But insiders, yeah, if you're into this game or into this instrument, this thing, you use very specific terms. For example, I say stamp collectors. If you're among stamp collectors, they have a language that the rest of us don't understand. So at this point, if I'm writing this, is this the time to use lingo or am I still on the no? I've also heard I should keep things simple. Keep it simple. You can use lingo if you want. You have to be absolutely positive that your market understands the lingo or the insider language or the secret codes or the slang that they use. Don't take it for granted. Make sure that everyone knows. For example, if you're going to bowlers, most bowlers know what a turkey is. It's three strikes in a row. It's a very common thing. However, there are other terms that not all bowlers know. Maybe they haven't heard of it if they're bowling in Wisconsin as opposed to Southern California. So be very careful of that. But even more important to that is that this stage for the USP, this is for you. This is for you to have as a foundation for you to be able to refer to, to have in your head, to position yourself to be able to start the conversations that are going to go on with your prospect. For example, getting back to the elevator speech or the talk, if you're in a bar and you overhear somebody that comes in and starts talking to the bartender. How would you approach? This is a pithy, straightforward way of without offending people without saying, I need five minutes, but if you'll give me five minutes, I'll explain to you who I am and what I have for you. Nobody's going to give you that. You can just say, hey, I overheard that you had that problem. I've been helping people with that problem solve it very quickly. And they've been getting great results much better than any of the alternatives out there. In fact, we've helped people, even if they are stubborn, stupid, have no money, have no idea what to do next, anything like that. So following this helps you have that very short, pithy conversation that gets a lot of information in there to somebody who says, oh, you've done this? Yes, that is the benefit that I would really like. Wow, I have gone through that worst situation or I know about that worst situation. So that then opens the door to the rest of the conversation that will follow. Okay, excellent. As I said, I'm going to wrap up the questions there because I think this one is just as simple. If you keep it this simple and don't try to put way more stuff into this one module than needs to be. So we'll wrap this up and let folks get to work on their exercise. And again, if you're in the coaching program, spend a little time thinking about this and then post it for your coach and get the feedback. Okay, here's the exercise. It's very simple. It's profound in the way it will set up everything else you do in this program. We help this group of people do this benefit or benefits better than your competitors or common wisdom, dot, dot, dot, even F and then give the worst situation possible. Have some fun with this. Have some experiment a little bit. Get outside of your comfort zone if you need to. Start thinking about what the real situations are that people get themselves into. Start thinking about the worst things that happen to your prospects. Start thinking about how you actually help people. What it is you do in a very short, succinct amount of time. And you'll be dealing with benefits which we're going to be talking about in a bit. So just think about benefits right now as a way of benefiting people. What it's going to do that makes them feel better, feel like they're getting further along, making more money, whatever it is they're doing, get this down. Just like in the bar room conversation or the elevator chat that you're having, this is a very short, pithy way to get right to the point. So give a stab at this. This is not something you do and then put away and never touch again. You will be evolving as forever if you're in business or if you're dealing with clients. Or however you are trying to get a sales message to the group of people you're after. This is going to help enormously.
